narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your bar exam outcome. For you specifically, it estimated a 61.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile of 1, which is in the bottom 10% compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. This low ranking signaled to the model a potential struggle with sustaining strong performance later in your education, a key predictor of bar success. Next in influence was your Year 1 school grade decile, also at 1—again well below the average—reinforcing concerns about early academic challenges that often carry over. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 provided some counterbalance, as it was close to the dataset average of 3.21 and nudged the prediction slightly toward passing by showing solid college-level capability. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 33 (below the average 36.7), lower-middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—which is common for 93% of applicants—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile stands out for those consistently low early deciles, which the model sees as the biggest hurdles, even with your respectable undergrad grades. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on patterns where similar profiles faced bar exam difficulties. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for students from similar backgrounds. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view in your favor, and many schools value persistence and growth stories like yours. Keep pushing—you've already shown resilience in completing undergrad successfully.
